title: Cat Licked Tea Tree Oil: Urgent Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Tea tree oil is highly toxic to cats. If your cat licked it, seek emergency veterinary care immediately. Learn about symptoms and triage steps.
content_html:
What is Tea Tree Oil Toxicity?

Tea tree oil, also known as Melaleuca oil, is an essential oil often used in human skincare for its antiseptic properties. However, it contains organic compounds called terpenes that are highly poisonous to cats. Because cats lack the specific liver enzymes required to break down these toxins, even a small amount licked from their fur or a spilled container can cause severe illness or central nervous system failure.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a critical emergency. Ingestion of tea tree oil requires immediate veterinary intervention. Toxicity can lead to rapid onset of tremors, seizures, and liver damage. You should contact your local emergency veterinarian or a pet poison helpline immediately. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen before seeking help.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of the product label is one of the most helpful things you can do for your vet. A photo allows the medical team to see the exact concentration of the oil (e.g., 100% pure vs. 5% dilution) and identify if there are other toxic additives. This information is vital for calculating the toxic dose and determining the fastest course of life-saving treatment for your cat.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of tea tree oil poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include drooling, vomiting, stumbling (ataxia), muscle tremors, low body temperature, and in severe cases, seizures or coma.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat water or milk to neutralize the oil?
faqs.answer: No. Do not give your cat anything by mouth without veterinary instruction, as this can increase the risk of aspiration or speed up the absorption of the toxin.

faqs.question: How is tea tree oil poisoning treated by a vet?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves decontamination, IV fluids to support the liver, and medications to control tremors or seizures.
